speaker: interviewer
text: --the time of the establishment of the ghetto.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Actually, it started in the-- in the fall of 1940. I remember it was pretty cold already. And we had to move out from our apartment. And we had to move out and move in-- into a designated area in Warsaw. We did not have any apartment to move to. And my father loaded up the furniture on a horse and wagon. It was a huge wagon because they tried to take all the furniture what they could to empty the apartment. And we moved into the ghetto. We moved into the ghetto. My father had a friend. And one-- I don't remember the street, but I remember it was a big one of these courts-- what I described prior-- building. And it had a court. And we unloaded the, the wagon with all the furniture. We spent the night in the court. We did not have a roof over our head. And my father started to look for a place to live. He found a place the following evening. That was somewhere on Smozca Street. There was a market on Smozca Street. I don't remember what number Smozca. He found a, a place. And we moved all our furniture to that place. It was really a small, little room. We hardly got the stuff in. And the ghetto was very crowded. It was just a lot of people walking, pushing little wagons, carrying things on their back. And we got to this little place. About a few days later, my father was called in on a job to open a-- some kind of a lock by a-- by some kind of a rich person. And when you open that lock in this building, which was on Chlodna Street, he saw that there was another empty apartment nearby, which he probably knew all the locks in town-- how to open them.

speaker: interviewee
text: So he opened the other apartment. He found that there was an empty place. And immediately after that, we loaded up our belongings. And we moved to this place.

speaker: interviewee
text: It was a huge apartment. I never seen an apartment like that. It was, like, maybe two or three rooms, probably belonged to a-- maybe a professional person. I don't know. It was even a telephone that-- I remember playing with that telephone, like I was one of the most important people calling people and receiving telephone calls. So it was huge, nice, and really pleasant. We moved in over there. And life started to be, you know, something like a new period for me. The most important thing was food. We, at that time, had some money saved up that my father saved for the purpose probably of opening up his dream business. And we started to go out of the ghetto at that time. The ghetto wasn't tight closed. It was just a, a policeman-- Polish policeman and a German staying by the gate-- designated area of the ghetto. And there was a, a, a gate that people could come in and come, come out, showing certain documents. And I remember my father used to come to the Polish policemen. He show him his identification as a invalid, and the policeman would kind of feel sorry for him. My father would pull out his eye-- his glass eye and kind of look pretty, pretty bad. And the policeman would let us out. The German didn't pay any attention at all.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we would go. I remember it was on Leszno. We went to the [NON-ENGLISH], they called it. That was a big market. And we bought some food. And we brought the food in. And that continued for few weeks.

speaker: interviewee
text: Then they closed up the ghetto. And nobody was able to get out of the ghetto. I mean, nobody, no adult would take a chance to go out. The ghetto was designated quite a few blocks around, cordoned off. And they built a, a high wall, probably about a 10-foot wall, with glass on top of the wall that you could not scale that wall. It was impossible to scale that wall. On the other hand, they left a little bit of a hole at the gutter for the water to run through. So when it rained, the water was running through. It was a small hole, but enough for me, as a little kid, to crawl through. So I wasn't the only one that was crawling through. Other kids would crawl through, not knowing what happens on the other side.

speaker: interviewee
text: So you made your way through. And I would go out from the ghetto-- I knew already the place that I went with my father to buy food-- and buy some food. I couldn't carry too much.

speaker: interviewee
text: I would buy one bread, sometimes some potatoes, and a little bit of different things what was pretty cheap, not to spend too much money, bring it into the ghetto. My father would sell it. And we had a little bit always left over from that.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, what time of day was it? And how were you--

speaker: interviewee
text: It was mostly--

speaker: interviewer
text: --able to get out?

speaker: interviewee
text: --in the daylight because there was a curfew. You couldn't do it at night. I don't remember exactly the hours of the curfew, if it started-- 6 o'clock. You know, actually, it would-- the day would start, like, 6 o'clock that the curfew was over. And then it continued to the evening. I don't remember. It was kind of-- the day was pretty short because winter.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I remember, like, it was getting dark. And I always tried to come back before, before the nightfall. But I don't remember what time the curfew actually started in the evening. How was it that you were able to get out without being seen in daylight?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I-- the wall was not watched by anybody because nobody expected anybody to crawl through that little hole.

speaker: interviewer
text: How big was the hole? I'm sorry.

speaker: interviewee
text: The, the hole must have been, I would say, maybe, like, I don't know, 20 inches by maybe 30 inches. Like, would be like 30 inches wide probably, maybe three foot wide, and maybe 15 to 20 inches high, just enough for the water to come through, but also enough for my head to go through and my shoulders. And once I was out, I did not have to come back through the same hole. Because coming back, you could come as a child to the gate, and they would let you in. All the children that were out during the day were able to come back to the ghetto at that time without getting beaten up in the beginning or anything, as long as you did not try to go out. Out was a problem. They would not let you out. In was much easier. You came in through the gate, whatever you carried with you. If you carried a disguise behind your coat or something, you were able to run into the ghetto without any problem. Now in the beginning, I remember I used to come up to the Polish policeman and tell him that my father was a caretaker of one of the buildings in ghetto. That was even before I was crawling out through the hole. So that was, like, the first few weeks, I was able to fool them. After my father was not able to go out, I would come to the Polish policeman. And I spoke a very clear Polish because thanks to the Polish kids that I was getting beaten up here and there, it did some good to me because I spoke a very clear Polish. And nobody could recognize me that I was a Jewish kid.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I would walk over to the policeman and tell him very simple that I live onto this and this address with my father, which is a caretaker and a Polack. And I have to go out and buy some food for my father. So he wouldn't do anything. He just let me go.

speaker: interviewee
text: The German didn't even pay any attention. There were Jewish policemen-- they call it the Jewish Militia that were staying on the Jewish side of the ghetto. And they would kind of screen everybody approaching the Polish policeman.

speaker: interviewee
text: So to them, I played the same game. I would come and say that I'm a Polack, and I've got to talk to the Polish policeman. And they didn't recognize me. Excuse me.

speaker: interviewee
text: There must-- there must have been other kids playing the same game like I did. I don't know. I probably wasn't the, the only one. But I know I was succeeding pretty well in it.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I would come and tell him the story. And they would let me go. I'd go out and buy my food.

speaker: interviewee
text: I even went out with my sister quite a few times in the beginning, playing the same game. She was also-- she was kind of dirty blond--looking, and she spoke as good Polish as I did. So we had no problem going out. Coming back, we had no problem getting in. And that's, like-- played it for quite a few weeks. Then things were already getting tougher and tougher. They wouldn't let us out anymore because I suppose these people working in the ghetto had to evacuate or something. Because I remember I came over once to a Polish policeman and tried to get out, and I got a good snap in my face for lying.

speaker: interviewee
text: So he didn't care if I was Jewish or Polish. As long as I was on the ghetto side, he assumed that I was a Jew and I was lying to him. And I did not take any more chances doing that. So I tried all kinds of tricks that I could to get out of the ghetto. Many times I'd get out walking alongside the wall and trying to sneak behind the backs of everybody. And that took quite a bit of sneaking through. So many times I would approach the gate getting by the Polish-- the Jewish policeman, getting by the Polish policemen. And there was a situation that they were bringing in into the ghetto carts of food, carts of different transportation. They were in and out, coming out with documents, and going in with documents.

speaker: interviewee
text: When they got busy-- I had to wait for a situation. When they got a little bit busy, a little commotion, I'd run through. So many times, I went through with my sister. Came to a point that it was too dangerous for my sister to do that because she was much bigger already. She had to wear a band-- an armband. I think the armband started from the age of 12 or 13. I don't remember exactly. But she had to wear the armband. And being caught without the armband could get beaten up or even locked up. So it was too dangerous for her. Besides that, we spent one night on the gentile side, on the Aryan side. Not always you were able to come into the ghetto. There were certain times that you came to the ghetto and they would not let you out-- I mean, they would not let you in. So we had to kind of find a pretty decent German that would let the kids in without any problem.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I remember one night, there was some kind of a inspection or something. You couldn't even get close to the ghetto. So we spent the whole night in a-- in an apartment house, in the hallway with my sister.

speaker: interviewee
text: And it was quite dangerous for her to be there. So my parents decided that she will not go out anymore. And I would be the only one that will go out if the possibility, the chance, came about. And I did sometimes, like, two-- sometimes even three rounds a day, I would go out of the ghetto. I had many ways. And I wasn't the only one because we kind of formed a group of children doing the contraband. I didn't know them personally, but I knew them by looking at them. And if they were carrying something out from-- into the ghetto, I could recognize them. They were my kind of kids.

speaker: interviewee
text: So if I saw one coming in, I would go over to him and asked him which way did you come in. Then he would tell me that this guard and this and this situation, he is-- he's OK, you know. You, you could go there. So I tried to go out through there, smuggle my way out, or go in different way through a hole or through other means of getting out of the ghetto, buying the food, and trying to come in through that gate. There was a Zabowska gate. And there was a Leszno gate.

speaker: interviewee
text: There-- I, I can't remember all the-- all the places. But there were quite a few around Warsaw that you were able to take a tram-- I mean a streetcar-- get to that point and come in.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long did this go on for where you're--

speaker: interviewee
text: This go-- this went--

speaker: interviewer
text: --smuggle in and out?

speaker: interviewee
text: --on in 1940, winter, into '41 and during the summer of '41. It kept on going on, I remember, through the whole summer into the fall of 1941. Things were getting really harder and harder of going-- getting out of the ghetto. And things were getting to the point that people were really starving from hunger. You could not find-- you know, if I can describe in brief the life in the ghetto. The life in the ghetto-- whoever had something to eat survived. Whoever did not have what to eat was dying of hunger.

speaker: interviewer
text: And things were-- in 1941 summer, you seen a lot of people laying in the streets, swollen from hunger. People were dying from hunger. Children would come-- like, you had beggars walking around and calling in the houses, can we get a-- in the beginning, can we get a, a small piece of bread from somebody? People would throw out of the window a piece of bread, a scrap of a potato.

speaker: interviewee
text: Later on, people did not-- the children would not ask for bread. They would ask like a little peels of potatoes-- if you can throw out of the window. Or they stayed in the streets in groups of two or three and just begged, you know, if somebody could give them something to eat. And you could pass by maybe two, three days later. And the same children would be laying on the sidewalk already swollen. And a lot of them died. And there was, like, a group of people that were taking the dead people away on wagons and carrying them out from the ghetto into the cemetery, that they called it, the [NON-ENGLISH] Cemetery in Warsaw, a huge cemetery. And that cemetery was kind of separated from the main portion of the ghetto. So they would let out the corpses to the cemetery, which was one of my escape routes also-- getting out into the cemetery, in through the cemetery, going out into the Aryan side.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I did all kinds of runs through the cemetery, getting over the cemetery. There was also a Polish cemetery on the other side. And there was a-- an area called [NON-ENGLISH], which was a field. I remember there was, like, two or three holes from 1939 of the bombing of Warsaw. There were huge holes inside. So I had to kind of walk around these holes to get through there to the market. And I was caught once over there and beaten up pretty bad from some Polacks that pulled my pants off and identified me that I was a Jew. Because by talking to me-- I always tried to connive my way out of the situation that I'm a Polack. And I do it for, for money.

speaker: interviewee
text: Most of them were taking the food away from us. Like, there were gangs of kids waiting on the Aryan side when you went outside and you tried to bring something in. So, in other words, you had a little sack on your back. And you would come to the gate and try to make your way to the ghetto. They would wait for you outside, two or three, in little groups. And when they saw you coming in, they have a-- they had a razor blade. And they cut the sack off, which you wore on your back, and kept their little basket or a sack underneath. And all the food would fall into their sack. And you wound up with a cut bag only. And you had nothing else. Well, you-- my mother sewed in-- into my coat-- that was winter of 1941-- not '40-- '41-- '40 to '41. Not '41 to '42, because I left already the ghetto at that time. It was in 1940. And she sewed in some pockets in my coat. And I was able to put bread in the pockets. But that was kind of weighing me down. So I couldn't walk very well. But I was able to carry two breads into the ghetto. And bringing in two breads, that was quite a arrangement because I could sell one, and the other one I was able to eat with my family.

speaker: interviewee
text: So anyway, there were all kinds of ways that I was able to, to smuggle things. At one time, I had an arrangements with my father that I would get on a streetcar that was running through the ghetto, and my father would wait at one point.

speaker: interviewee
text: And the back-- you know, like a streetcar, was-- on one side was a policeman with the steps which you walked up. The tramway, the streetcar, came to the ghetto stop to the gate. And there was a policeman that got on, on the tramway, on the streetcar. And nobody was able to get off in the ghetto. And the tram, or the streetcar, was going through the ghetto because they couldn't cut off the line. So they let the tramway, the streetcar, cut-- travel through the ghetto. And then at the other end, the policeman would get off. And people continued. All the passengers were Polish passengers that were just crossing the ghetto.

speaker: interviewee
text: So what I would do, I'd get on the tram-- on this streetcar-- and I had a little bag arranged sometimes with some cereal in that bag. Sometimes was a, a small bread inside, as much as I could-- I could carry. And the back of the-- where the policeman was, the streetcar was built in a way that you could open it in both ways.

speaker: interviewee
text: But sometimes, they only had it open on one side, and it was like an iron gate-- and the back of the streetcar from the opposite side where the policeman would get off. So the gate had like big openings, like maybe, I don't know, a foot wide or something. And I remember when I made up with my father, that they would wait for me, I'd push a package through that opening. And my father would catch it on the other side. So this continued for a while-- all kinds of situations just to be able to bring in something to the ghettos. And we were able to sell it and eat from it. I remember one time, I even made my way out of the ghetto by-- in-- there used to be a situation when two streetcars would meet right at the gate. And between the two streetcars, there was a space, maybe like 2 foot wide or so, that nobody would suspect that somebody would go through. But this situation, I had to wait sometimes for hours until two streetcars would meet right at the gate. Then I would run through-- between the two streetcars when they stood. And one policeman would get on it. The other policeman would get off because they were running in two opposite ways, the, the streetcars.

speaker: interviewee
text: So between the two street cars, I would run through and run out of the ghetto. And by the time the, the street cars would start moving, I would be out of the ghetto. And nobody expected somebody to run because it was really dangerous. You could touch-- you know, you could touch the car with your shoulder. But I guess I was pretty small that I always managed to get out without getting, you know, kind of rubbed off by the streetcar or anything like that.

speaker: interviewee
text: Except one time, a policeman waited for me on the outside. When I came on the other side-- he must have seen me running or something. And he started to beat me up with a rubber hose. And I started to run back into the ghetto. And he kept on running after me, hitting me in the head. And by the time I got out back into the ghetto, I passed out. And I don't know how long I was laying there, but I remember it was already pretty dark when I came to myself. But there was no surprise a kid laying in the street. There were a lot of kids laying in the street.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I kind of came back to myself. And I got up. And I made my way home. It was pretty late in the evening already by the time I got there. One of these situation that I was able to get in and out.

speaker: interviewee
text: And this continued like that until, I would say, the end of 1941. It was getting in-- into the end of the year. And things were getting really bad. I mean, I could not get out that often. It was very hard. A lot of kids would get really beaten up if they caught them. And it was getting harder and harder. My father decided that we are going to leave the Warsaw Ghetto, And we're going to go to the town where he comes from. By the way, my grandparents passed away right in the beginning of 1941. In the winter '40 to '41, they passed away. My uncles kept-- still alive. There was, actually, on my mother's side, the uncle that was the painter and the uncle that was married to my aunt-- actually, the barber. He was alive. My uncle the painter was alive. The rest of my uncles actually ran away in 1939. They ran away to Russia. They escaped the war of-- actually, they escaped the Germans by going to Russia. So that's a different situation altogether.

speaker: interviewer
text: So when did your family now go to your father's hometown?

speaker: interviewee
text: OK. So here, we were four people trying to get out of the ghetto-- my mother and father, my sister, and myself. My sister was chosen the first one to go. And she-- at that time, I found out. Because until that time, I didn't know that my uncle that was the painter became a superintendent of this project, of this building over there. And he was taking care of the building. And on the roof, they had contraband going through the roof.

speaker: interviewee
text: So until that point, I didn't know what he was doing. We didn't see each other. And I didn't know that it was possible through his roof to get out on the Polish side. So they decided that my sister will go one evening to my uncle, and she'll make her way through the roof, and go to the town where my father comes from, to Siedlce, which was 100 kilometers, took about three hours by train to get there.

speaker: interviewee
text: But she had no problem getting any place. She spoke good Polish. So she made her way to my uncle. And a few weeks later, we got a-- we got words that she made it to the town of Siedlce. And she got over there. She was safe and everything. The next one, I had to go. Now, I was too small to travel alone at that time. So they decided that my aunt, the barber's wife, would travel with me. Now, she was also a very fair complexion, kind of blondish looking, spoke very well Polish. So there was no danger for her to go with me or for me to go with her. She was a perfect situation for both of us to go out. We went out. In the evening, we came to my uncle. We got to the roof. We made our way through the roof. And we went into a building on the other side. And we waited in the staircase until dawn, until the curfew would, would finish. So I remember it was very quiet, but kind of scary, you know. I would probably prefer to be in the street than sitting in that stairwell over there. But we got through that night. In the morning, we started to walk toward the railroad station. It was the Warszawa Glowna, what they call it in Polish, the general-- the main central railroad of Warsaw-- railroad station. And we got over there. There were thousands of people traveling in all kinds of directions. And we got in line. And we waited to, to get a ticket. It was really hard to get a ticket. People were pushing. And there were Polish policeman and a German policeman-- black uniform. That-- they were kind of keeping order and keeping the line going. And there was always somebody that cut, cut in. Whatever it was, I stood there for a couple hours with my aunt trying to get to buy a ticket. And a German [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] policeman, the one that was from the railroad-- the black uniforms-- they called them Bahnschutz. He kind of started to play around with my aunt, flirting with her. I don't know. They-- she made believe she don't understand what he's talking. And he couldn't speak Polish. But he was kind of getting, getting cute with her. And the Polish policeman occasionally would come and translate for them words here and there. But he wouldn't leave her alone.

speaker: interviewee
text: So after a while, she told me that she-- she'll be right back. She walked away. And I never seen her again. She just disappeared in the railroad station.

speaker: interviewee
text: And here, me being like, at that time, 10 years old, I had to-- I had some money on me that my father gave me besides what my aunt supposed to buy the tickets. I got to my, my-- next to the cashier, and I bought a ticket. And he kind of looked at me funny, a kid my age going to this-and-this town. I had to explain it, why am I going alone. Anyway, I got there. And I bought a ticket. And we went.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. We have to change to the next--

speaker: interviewee
text: OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: --tape now.
